fun_fact: The UK consumes over 100 million cups of tea every single day.
French was the official language of the UK for about 300 years.
Big Ben is the name of the bell inside the tower, not the clock or the tower itself.
The shortest scheduled flight in the world is in Scotland, lasting just 47 seconds.
Windsor Castle is the oldest and largest occupied castle in the world.
The unicorn is the national animal of Scotland.
It is technically illegal to enter the Houses of Parliament wearing a suit of armor.
London has the world's oldest underground railway system, the 'Tube'.
The British Library is the largest library in the world by number of items cataloged.
Champagne was actually invented in England by scientist Christopher Merret in 1662.
The Queen (and now the King) does not need a passport to travel abroad.
There are more chickens in the UK than there are people.
The BBC is the world's oldest national broadcasting organization.
London has over 170 museums, many of which are free to the public.
The first-ever postage stamp, the Penny Black, was issued in the UK in 1840.
Spain is the world's largest producer of olive oil, accounting for about 44% of global supply.
The world's oldest existing restaurant, Sobrino de Botín (est. 1725), is in Madrid.
Spain has more bars per inhabitant than any other country in the European Union.
There is no tooth fairy in Spain; instead, a mouse named 'Ratoncito Pérez' collects teeth.
Spain was the first country in the world to use wind energy as its primary electricity source.
The Spanish National Anthem has no official lyrics.
The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be built in Barcelona, but the city rejected it.
Spain remains the only European country to have a physical border with an African country (Ceuta/Melilla).
The 'Tomatina' festival involves thousands of people throwing tomatoes at each other for fun.
Spain has the second-highest number of UNESCO World Heritage Cities in the world.
The stapler was invented in the Basque Country of Spain in the 18th century.
The Spanish New Year tradition involves eating 12 grapes at the stroke of midnight.
Spain didn't officially join World War I or World War II, remaining neutral.
Spanish is the second most spoken native language in the world, after Mandarin.
The Sagrada Família in Barcelona has been under construction for over 140 years.
There are more bicycles in the Netherlands than there are people.
Dutch men are the tallest in the world, with an average height of 6 feet (183 cm).
The Netherlands was the first country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage (2001).
One-third of the Netherlands is located below sea level.
The Dutch national anthem, the 'Wilhelmus,' is the oldest in the world (written in 1568).
Carrots were originally purple or white; the Dutch bred them to be orange in honor of the House of Orange.
Amsterdam is built entirely on wooden poles (the Royal Palace sits on 13,659 poles).
The Netherlands is the world's largest exporter of flowers, specifically tulips.
Gin was invented in the Netherlands (known as Jenever) before becoming popular in England.
The Dutch consume the most licorice in the world—about 4 lbs per person per year.
Schiphol Airport is situated 4 meters below sea level on what used to be a lake.
The microscope and the telescope were both invented in the Netherlands.
There are over 1,000 historic windmills still standing in the country.
The Dutch invented the first stock exchange in the world in Amsterdam (1602).
It is a tradition to eat 'Haring' (raw herring) with onions and pickles as a snack.
Belgium has the world record for the longest time without a government (541 days).
The Big Bang Theory was actually proposed by a Belgian priest, Georges Lemaître.
Belgium produces more than 800 different varieties of beer.
The saxophone was invented in Belgium by Adolphe Sax in the 1840s.
Belgium has the highest number of castles per square mile in the world.
Brussels' Zaventem airport is the largest chocolate selling point in the world.
French fries are actually Belgian; they were named 'French' by US soldiers in WWI.
The world's first printed newspaper was published in Antwerp in 1605.
Belgium has three official languages: Dutch, French, and German.
The 'Manneken Pis' statue in Brussels has a wardrobe of over 1,000 different outfits.
Belgium is the birthplace of the Smurfs (created by cartoonist Peyo).
The Body Mass Index (BMI) was developed by the Belgian mathematician Adolphe Quetelet.
Belgium is home to Tomorrowland, the world's largest electronic dance music festival.
Napoleon was finally defeated at the Battle of Waterloo, located in Belgium.
The world's longest tram line is the Belgian Coast Tram, which is 68 km long.
Norway knighted a penguin named Sir Nils Olav who lives in the Edinburgh Zoo.
The Nobel Peace Prize is the only Nobel prize awarded in Oslo; the others are in Sweden.
Norway introduced salmon sushi to the Japanese in the 1980s.
Modern skiing was invented in Norway (the word 'ski' is Old Norse for 'piece of wood').
Norway has the world's longest road tunnel, the Lærdal Tunnel (24.5 km).
Grandiosa frozen pizza is so popular it is often called the unofficial national dish of Norway.
The world's most remote island, Bouvet Island, is a Norwegian territory.
Norway has more Winter Olympic medals than any other nation in the world.
Gasoline prices in Norway are among the highest in the world, despite being a major oil exporter.
In a small town called Hell, Norway, the temperature regularly freezes over in winter.
Norway has a land border with Russia that is 196 km long.
Norwegians are the world's second-largest consumers of coffee per capita.
The 'Svalbard Global Seed Vault' protects the world's crop diversity against global disasters.
Norway's coastline is so jagged that if stretched out, it would circle the Earth twice.
It is illegal to declaw your cat in Norway unless it is medically necessary.
Sweden has more islands than any other country in the world, with over 267,000.
The world-famous furniture store IKEA was founded in Sweden in 1943.
Sweden was the first country in the world to ban spanking and corporal punishment of children (1979).
The Nobel Prize ceremonies (except the Peace Prize) are held in Stockholm every year.
Sweden imports trash from other countries because it is so efficient at recycling it for energy.
The 'Lagom' philosophy, meaning 'not too much, not too little, just right,' is central to Swedish culture.
The pacemaker, the ultrasound, and the three-point seatbelt were all invented in Sweden.
Sweden has a 'Right of Public Access' (Allemansrätten) allowing anyone to camp or hike almost anywhere.
North Korea owes Sweden over 300 million dollars for 1,000 Volvo cars stolen in the 1970s.
Abba is Sweden's most successful musical export of all time.
Stockholm’s subway system is often called the world’s longest art gallery.
Swedes are among the world's highest consumers of coffee.
The Swedish monarchy is one of the oldest in the world, dating back over 1,000 years.
Surströmming, a fermented herring, is so smelly that some airlines ban it on flights.
The official Twitter account of Sweden was managed by a different random citizen every week for seven years.
Finland has been named the happiest country in the world for seven consecutive years.
There are more saunas in Finland than there are cars (about 3 million saunas).
Finland was the first country in Europe to give women the right to vote (1906).
The Finnish language is not Indo-European; it is part of the Uralic family, related to Estonian and Hungarian.
Finland holds the World Championships for Wife Carrying and Mobile Phone Throwing.
The official 'home' of Santa Claus is in Rovaniemi, Finnish Lapland.
Finland has approximately 188,000 lakes within its borders.
Finns drink more coffee per capita than any other nation in the world.
Heavy metal is extremely popular; Finland has the most metal bands per capita in the world.
Angry Birds and Clash of Clans were both created by Finnish companies.
The northern lights (Aurora Borealis) are visible in Finnish Lapland about 200 nights a year.
In Finland, speeding tickets are calculated based on the offender's annual income.
The only Finnish word to make it into the English language is 'sauna'.
Finland has an annual 'Day for Failure' to celebrate setbacks as learning experiences.
Finland is the only country in the world to broadcast news in Latin (Nuntii Latini).
The Danish flag, the Dannebrog, is the oldest national flag in continuous use (since 1219).
Lego was invented in Denmark, and the name comes from 'Leg Godt,' meaning 'Play Well'.
Denmark has no mountains; the highest 'peak' is only 170 meters above sea level.
Copenhagen is one of the most bicycle-friendly cities; 50% of residents commute by bike.
The Danish monarchy is the oldest uninterrupted monarchy in Europe.
There is no word for 'please' in the Danish language.
Danish pastry actually originated in Austria; Danes call it 'Viennese bread' (wienerbrød).
Denmark is consistently ranked as one of the least corrupt countries in the world.
You are never more than 50 kilometers away from the sea in Denmark.
Bluetooth technology was named after the 10th-century Danish King Harald 'Bluetooth' Gormsson.
Tivoli Gardens in Copenhagen is one of the oldest amusement parks in the world.
Denmark has 444 islands, but only about 76 of them are inhabited.
The Little Mermaid statue in Copenhagen has been decapitated twice by vandals.
Handball was invented in Denmark in the late 19th century.
The concept of 'Hygge' (coziness and comfortable conviviality) is a core part of Danish identity.
Iceland has no mosquitoes at all due to its unique climate and soil chemistry.
Icelanders do not have surnames; they use a patronymic system (father's name + son or dóttir).
A majority of Icelanders believe in the existence of elves (Huldufólk).
Iceland is home to the world’s oldest parliament, the Althing, established in 930 AD.
There are no McDonald's restaurants in the entire country of Iceland.
Iceland produces 100% of its electricity from renewable sources (geothermal and hydro).
The country has more authors per capita than any other nation; 1 in 10 Icelanders will publish a book.
Beer was illegal in Iceland until 1989.
Iceland has 13 'Santa Clauses' known as the Yule Lads, who are mischievous trolls.
The Icelandic police do not carry guns.
Iceland has a 'Naming Committee' that must approve all new baby names to preserve the language.
Babies in Iceland are frequently left outside in strollers to nap in the cold air.
The country is located on two tectonic plates (North American and Eurasian) that are pulling apart.
There are more sheep in Iceland than there are people.
Iceland is the only place in the world where you can dive between two continental plates (Silfra Fissure).
Switzerland has 4 official languages: German, French, Italian, and Romansh.
It is illegal to own just one guinea pig in Switzerland because they are social animals.
Switzerland has enough nuclear fallout shelters to accommodate its entire population.
The Swiss eat more chocolate than any other nation, averaging 11kg per person annually.
Switzerland hasn't been in a state of war since 1815.
Coffee in Zurich is among the most expensive in the world.
Switzerland is home to over 7,000 lakes.
The Swiss Flag is square, not rectangular (only the Vatican also has a square flag).
CERN, the world's largest particle physics laboratory, is located on the Swiss-French border.
In Switzerland, citizens can challenge any law passed by parliament if they gather 50,000 signatures.
The Swiss military keeps prepared 'destruction' points on all bridges and tunnels for defense.
Switzerland has more peaks over 4,000 meters than any other European country.
The Red Cross was founded in Geneva, Switzerland, in 1863.
Assisted suicide is legal in Switzerland and has been since 1942.
Switzerland produces about 450 varieties of cheese.
The oldest zoo in the world, Tiergarten Schönbrunn (est. 1752), is in Vienna.
Austria is the birthplace of many famous composers, including Mozart, Haydn, and Strauss.
The croissant was actually invented in Vienna (the Kipferl) before being popularized in France.
Austria is the only EU country that is not a member of NATO.
The world's largest emerald (2,860 carats) is kept in the Imperial Treasury in Vienna.
About 62% of Austria’s total land area is covered by the Alps.
The sewing machine was invented by an Austrian, Josef Madersperger, in 1814.
Vienna has been voted the world's most livable city multiple times.
The 'Sound of Music' was filmed in Salzburg, but many locals have never seen the movie.
Austria was the first country in the world to make organic farming a national regulation.
The world's oldest operating restaurant, St. Peter Stiftskeller (est. 803 AD), is in Salzburg.
Arnold Schwarzenegger was born and raised in Thal, Austria.
Postcards were first used and issued in Austria in 1869.
The Austrian flag is one of the oldest national flags in the world.
Vienna has a massive 'Coffee House Culture' that is listed as a UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage.
Halloween originated in Ireland from the ancient Celtic festival of Samhain.
There are no wild snakes in Ireland; they never migrated there after the last Ice Age.
The harp is the national symbol of Ireland, making it the only country with a musical instrument as an emblem.
St. Patrick, the patron saint of Ireland, was actually British, not Irish.
Ireland has won the Eurovision Song Contest a record 7 times.
More Irish people live outside of Ireland (the diaspora) than inside the country.
Guinness beer was founded by Arthur Guinness, who signed a 9,000-year lease for the brewery.
Ireland is known as the 'Emerald Isle' because of its lush green landscape and high rainfall.
The 'Wild Atlantic Way' in Ireland is the longest defined coastal driving route in the world.
Hook Lighthouse in Wexford is one of the oldest working lighthouses in the world (800+ years).
The Titanic was built in Belfast, Northern Ireland.
Ireland was the first country in the world to legalize same-sex marriage by popular vote.
Irish is the first official language of Ireland, though English is more widely spoken.
The White House in Washington D.C. was designed by an Irishman, James Hoban.
The oldest pub in Ireland, Sean's Bar, dates back to 900 AD.
Portugal is the world’s largest producer of cork, providing over 50% of the global supply.
The oldest bookstore in the world, Livraria Bertrand (est. 1732), is in Lisbon.

The University of Coimbra, established in 1290, is one of the oldest in the world.
Portugal and England have the oldest active diplomatic alliance in the world (Treaty of Windsor, 1386).
Fado is the traditional, soulful music of Portugal and is a UNESCO World Heritage treasure.
The Vasco da Gama Bridge in Lisbon is one of the longest bridges in Europe.
Portugal was the first country in the world to decriminalize the possession of all drugs (in 2001).
The westernmost point of mainland Europe, Cabo da Roca, is located in Portugal.
Portuguese is the official language of 9 countries across 4 continents.
The biggest wave ever surfed was in Nazaré, Portugal (over 24 meters high).

Portugal has 300 days of sunshine per year on average.
Poland is home to the world's largest castle by land area, Malbork Castle.

Poland has one of the world's oldest salt mines, the Wieliczka Salt Mine.

Poland has one of the most diverse environments in Europe, from beaches to mountains and wetlands.
The oldest restaurant in Europe, Piwnica Świdnicka (est. 1273), is located in Wrocław.

Pierogi are so beloved that they have their own dedicated festival in Kraków.

The Czech Republic has the highest beer consumption per capita in the world.

The Mushroom hunting season is a national passion; almost every family goes 'foraging'.

The Charles University in Prague (est. 1348) is the oldest university in Central Europe.
The Czech Republic is consistently ranked among the top 10 safest countries globally.
The sugar cube was invented in the Czech town of Dačice in 1843.
Prague is known as the 'City of a Hundred Spires,' though it now has over 500.
Ice hockey is the most popular sport, and the national team is a global powerhouse.
The Hungarian Parliament Building is the third-largest parliament building in the world.
Budapest has the highest number of thermal springs of any capital city in the world.
The Rubik’s Cube was invented by Hungarian sculptor Ernő Rubik in 1974.

It is traditionally considered rude to clink beer glasses in Hungary.

Hungary has won the second-most gold medals in the Summer Olympics per capita.
The ballpoint pen (Biro) was invented by Hungarian journalist László Bíró.

Budapest has the oldest electrified underground railway system in mainland Europe.
Hungary is one of the oldest countries in Europe, founded in 895 AD.
The Dohány Street Synagogue in Budapest is the largest synagogue in Europe.
Paprika is the most essential ingredient in Hungarian cuisine.

Athens is one of the oldest cities in the world, with recorded history going back 3,400 years.
Greece has over 6,000 islands and islets, but only about 200 are inhabited.
No point in Greece is more than 85 miles (137 km) away from the sea.
Greece is the world's leading producer of sea sponges.
The first Olympic Games were held in 776 BC in Olympia.
Approximately 80% of Greece is covered by mountains.

Greece is the third-largest producer of olives in the world.

Romania is home to the largest population of brown bears in Europe (outside Russia).
The name 'Romania' comes from the Latin word 'Romanus,' meaning 'citizen of Rome'.
The Transfăgărășan road is often called the most beautiful driving road in the world.

Timișoara was the first city in Europe to have electric street lighting (1884).

Nadia Comăneci was the first gymnast to be awarded a perfect score of 10.0 in the Olympics.

Bucharest is often nicknamed 'Little Paris' because of its elegant architecture.
Peleș Castle was the first European castle to be entirely lit by electricity.
Bulgaria is the oldest country in Europe that hasn't changed its name since 681 AD.

The world's oldest gold treasure (over 6,000 years old) was found in Varna, Bulgaria.

Slovakia has more than 6,000 caves, including unique aragonite and ice caves.
Slovakia has the highest number of castles and chateaux per capita in the world.
Bratislava is the only capital city in the world that borders two independent countries (Austria and Hungary).

The village of Čičmany is famous for its folk architecture with white geometric patterns.

Slovakia’s national dish is Bryndzové Halušky (potato dumplings with sheep cheese).

Slovakia has nine national parks and 14 protected landscape areas.

The first technical university in the world was founded in Banská Štiavnica in 1762.

The world's oldest vine (over 400 years old) is still producing grapes in Maribor.
Slovenia is the only country with the word 'love' in its name.

Beekeeping is a national obsession; there are roughly 5 beekeepers for every 1,000 people.
The world's oldest wheel (5,150 years old) was discovered in the Ljubljana Marshes.
Slovenia has a diverse landscape that includes the Alps, the Mediterranean, and the Pannonian Plain.
Lake Bled features the only island in Slovenia, which is home to a famous church.

The capital city, Ljubljana, means 'The Loved One'.
Mount Triglav is the national symbol and appears on the country's coat of arms.
Slovenia has one of the highest numbers of Olympic medals per capita.
The Solkan Bridge has the largest stone arch among all railway bridges in the world.

Estonia was the first country in the world to adopt online political voting in 2005.
Skype was created by Estonian developers in 2003.
Estonia has two UNESCO World Heritage sites: the Old Town of Tallinn and the Struve Geodetic Arc.
The country has over 2,200 islands in the Baltic Sea.
Estonia is one of the least religious countries in the world.

Estonia has a 99.8% literacy rate, one of the highest in the world.

Latvia's flag is one of the oldest in the world, dating back to the 13th century.

Basketball is considered the unofficial national sport of Latvia.
Lithuanian is one of the oldest living languages in the world, closely related to Sanskrit.
The 'Stelmužė Oak' is one of the oldest trees in Europe, believed to be 1,500 years old.

Storks are the national bird and are considered a sign of good luck and harmony.
Ukraine is the largest country located entirely within the borders of Europe.

The world's largest concentration of mammoth bones was found in Ukraine.
Ukraine is the world's largest producer of sunflower oil.
The trembita is a traditional Ukrainian mountain horn and the world's longest wind instrument.
Ukrainian is often cited as one of the most melodic languages in the world.
The Vyshyvanka (embroidered shirt) is the most iconic piece of Ukrainian national clothing.
The necktie (cravat) originated in Croatia during the 17th century.
Croatia has over 1,200 islands, but only 48 are permanently inhabited.
The Dalmatian dog breed is named after the Croatian region of Dalmatia.

The Roman amphitheater in Pula is one of the best-preserved in the world.
White House stone: Some of the stone used to build the White House came from the island of Brač.

Croatia has more than 10% of its land protected as national or nature parks.

Zlatni Rat beach is famous because it changes shape depending on the wind and currents.

Serbia is the world's largest exporter of raspberries, accounting for about 30% of global supply.

Serbia is the only country outside of Russia that uses the Cyrillic and Latin alphabets equally.

Constantine the Great, the first Christian Roman Emperor, was born in Niš, Serbia.
Serbia has the largest Orthodox church in the Balkans, the Temple of Saint Sava.

Serbian scientist Mihajlo Pupin was a founding member of NASA (then NACA).
The 'Morava' school of architecture is a unique Serbian medieval style.
When Albanians shake their head from left to right, they mean 'yes'; a nod means 'no'.
Albania has over 170,000 concrete bunkers built during the Cold War.
In the early evening, Albanians take part in 'Xhiro', an official walk to socialize.
Mother Teresa is the only Albanian Nobel Laureate.
The country's energy production is 100% reliable on hydropower.
Albania was the first country in the world to officially declare itself an atheist state (1967).
There are more Albanians living outside the country than inside it.
The 'Code of Besa' is a traditional pledge of honor to protect guests at all costs.
The official name for Albania is 'Shqipëria', which means 'Land of the Eagles'.
Albania was never part of the Soviet Union or Yugoslavia; it was uniquely isolated.
Lake Ohrid, shared with North Macedonia, is one of the oldest lakes in Europe.
The national drink is Raki, a potent spirit usually made from grapes.
Private car ownership was illegal in Albania until the fall of communism in 1991.
Albania has 13 tiny islands, but only two are larger than one square kilometer.
The Albanian language is a unique branch of the Indo-European family with no close relatives.
Around 40% of Belarus is covered by dense forests, often called the 'Lungs of Europe'.
The European Bison, the continent's heaviest land animal, is the national symbol.
The National Library of Belarus is shaped like a rhombicuboctahedron (a complex diamond shape).
Belarusians have over 300 different recipes for potato dishes.
Independence Avenue in Minsk is one of the longest streets in Europe at 15 km.
Belarus is the only country in Europe that still carries out the death penalty.
The country is home to the Białowieża Forest, the last primeval forest in Europe.
Belarus has a 'Pancake Week' (Maslenitsa) where people eat pancakes to welcome spring.

Minsk has been destroyed and rebuilt 18 times throughout history.

Whistling inside a house is considered bad luck; it is believed to 'blow your money away'.
The 'World of Tanks' video game was created by a Belarusian company (Wargaming).

The Belarusian language has two different alphabets: Cyrillic and Latin (though Latin is rare).
Sarajevo hosted the first-ever Winter Olympics in a communist country (1984).
The country is often called the 'Heart-Shaped Land' because of its geographical shape.
The Stari Most (Old Bridge) in Mostar is a UNESCO site and a symbol of Balkan reconciliation.
Bosnia is home to the last remaining jungle in Europe, the Perućica Rainforest.
Sarajevo was the first city in Europe to have a full-time electric tram system (1885).
There are three presidents in Bosnia and Herzegovina, representing the three main ethnic groups.
The national anthem of Bosnia and Herzegovina has no official lyrics.
The country is home to the 'Bosnian Pyramids', which some claim are the oldest in the world.
Coffee is a way of life here; Bosnia has the 10th highest coffee consumption in the world.
The 'Hajdučka Republika Mijata Tomića' is a self-proclaimed micronation within its borders.
The currency is the 'Convertible Mark', which cannot be exchanged outside the country.

The 'Golden Lily' is the traditional national symbol of the Bosnian people.
Bosnia has a tiny 20 km stretch of coastline on the Adriatic Sea.
Traditional 'Sevdalinka' is a unique style of melancholy folk music native to Bosnia.
Cyprus is the first country in the world to put its map on its national flag.

The world's oldest wine still in production, Commandaria, comes from Cyprus.
Nicosia is the only divided capital city in the world (split between Greek and Turkish sides).

Cyprus enjoys an average of 320 days of sunshine every year.
The entire city of Paphos is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
The world's oldest perfumes were discovered by archaeologists in Cyprus.
Cyprus is one of the few places in the world where you can ski in the morning and swim in the afternoon.

The Neolithic settlement of Choirokoitia is one of the best-preserved prehistoric sites in the Mediterranean.
Cyprus has some of the cleanest beaches in Europe, consistently winning Blue Flag awards.
The 'Wish Tree' in Paphos is covered in tied ribbons believed to bring good luck.
Mount Olympus is the highest point in Cyprus, located in the Troodos Mountains.
Cyprus is geographically in Asia but is culturally and politically European.
Kosovo is the youngest country in Europe, having declared independence in 2008.
More than 70% of the population is under the age of 35, making it the 'youngest' population in Europe.

Kosovo is not in the EU, but it uses the Euro as its official currency.

The 'Marble Cave' in Gadime is a rare geological wonder made entirely of marble.
A street in the capital city of Pristina is named after Bill Clinton, complete with a statue.

The 'Newborn' monument in Pristina is repainted every year to celebrate independence.

The city of Prizren is known as the 'Museum City' due to its well-preserved Ottoman architecture.
Bear Sanctuary Prishtina provides a home for bears that were previously kept as restaurant attractions.
Rugova Canyon is one of Europe's longest and deepest canyons.
White-water rafting and hiking are major emerging tourist activities in the Accursed Mountains.
Luxembourg is the only Grand Duchy in the world (ruled by a Grand Duke).
It was the first country in the world to make all public transport free.
Luxembourg has the highest minimum wage in the European Union.
Nearly half of the country's population consists of foreign residents.
Luxembourgish is the national language, but most people are trilingual (French and German).
The capital, Luxembourg City, is built on massive fortifications and tunnels called the Casemates.
Luxembourg has won the Eurovision Song Contest five times.

There are more than 70 castles still standing in this small country.
The average Luxembourgish citizen owns more cars than anyone else in the EU.

Luxembourg is one of the safest countries in the world.
The country has the highest concentration of Michelin-starred restaurants per capita.
The national motto is 'Mir wëlle bleiwe wat mir sinn' (We want to remain what we are).
Malta is one of the most densely populated countries in the world.
The capital, Valletta, was the first ever planned city in Europe (built in 1566).
Malta has 365 churches—one for every day of the year.
The 'Popeye Village' film set from the 1980 movie is still a major tourist attraction.
Malta has no forests, no mountains, and no permanent rivers.
The Megalithic Temples of Malta are older than the Egyptian Pyramids and Stonehenge.
Malta was awarded the George Cross for its bravery during World War II.

Malta is a top filming location for movies like 'Gladiator' and 'Game of Thrones'.
The national dish is 'Stuffat tal-Fenek' (rabbit stew).

The Milestii Mici wine cellar is the largest in the world, containing over 2 million bottles.
Moldova is the least visited country in Europe by tourists.
The official language is Romanian, but many people call it 'Moldovan'.
The country is shaped like a bunch of grapes, which is fitting as it's a major wine producer.
Moldova is the only country in Europe with a negative population growth rate.
The national animal is the Aurochs (an extinct wild ox) featured on the flag.
Moldova is landlocked but is only 2 kilometers away from the Black Sea at its nearest point.
The village of Soroca is known as the 'Gypsy Capital' of the world for its ornate mansions.

The Cricova winery is so large it has underground 'streets' named after wine types (e.g., Cabernet St.).

The traditional musical instrument is the 'Nai' (pan flute).
Moldova has over 3,000 rivers, but only the Dniester and Prut are large.

The name 'Montenegro' means 'Black Mountain'.

The Tara River Canyon is the deepest canyon in Europe and the second deepest in the world.

The beach 'Velika Plaža' is one of the longest sandy beaches in Europe at 13 km.
North Macedonia is the only country to gain independence from Yugoslavia without any bloodshed.

Russia is the largest country in the world, covering over 17 million square kilometers.

Russia has more time zones (11) than any other country in the world.

The Louvre is the most visited art museum in the world.

There are over 1,600 distinct types of French cheese.

Turning a baguette upside down is considered unlucky in French culture.

Donaudampfschifffahrtselektrizitätenhaupt-betriebswerkbauunterbeamtengesellschaft is one of the longest words ever.

Italy has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country (59).

It is considered bad luck to place bread upside down on a table.

Istanbul is the only city in the world located on two continents (Europe and Asia).

Azerbaijan is known as the 'Land of Fire' due to its natural gas fires that burn eternally.

Georgia is one of the oldest wine-producing regions in the world, dating back 8,000 years.

Armenia was the first country in the world to adopt Christianity as its state religion (301 AD).

The Areni-1 winery is the oldest winery ever discovered, dating back 6,100 years.
There are more Armenians living outside Armenia (about 7 million) than inside (3 million).

Kazakhstan is the largest landlocked country in the world.

Apples originated in the wild forests of Kazakhstan; the city name 'Almaty' means 'city of apples'.

The capital city, Astana, was known as Nur-Sultan for a few years before reverting in 2022.

Kazakhstan is the world's largest producer of uranium.
The Medeu Skating Rink is the highest outdoor speed skating rink in the world.
Kazakhstan is transcontinental, with about 10% of its land located in Europe.
Liechtenstein is the world's leading producer of false teeth.

Liechtenstein is one of only two 'doubly landlocked' countries in the world.
The national anthem has the same melody as the United Kingdom's 'God Save the King'.
On the national holiday, the Prince invites all citizens to a garden party at Vaduz Castle.
There is only one natural lake in the entire country, the Gampriner Seele.
The Swiss military has accidentally 'invaded' Liechtenstein several times during training exercises.
Liechtenstein is the only country in the world named after the family that owns it.
You can 'rent' the entire country for events for roughly $70,000 a night.
Liechtenstein has the highest GDP per capita in the world when adjusted for purchasing power.
The country has no airport; the nearest major one is in Zurich, Switzerland.
Liechtenstein is a major producer of high-quality postage stamps for collectors.

Women in Liechtenstein did not get the right to vote until 1984.
Crime is so low that many residents don't even lock their front doors.
Monaco is the second smallest country in the world after Vatican City.
Citizens of Monaco (Monégasques) are forbidden by law from gambling at the casino.
Monaco has the world's highest life expectancy at approximately 89 years.
Monaco is so small you can walk across the entire country in about 56 minutes.
Monaco has more millionaires per capita than any other country in the world.
The Monaco Grand Prix is one of the oldest and most prestigious races in Formula One.
Monaco has no income tax for its residents.
The country has the world's highest police presence per capita.
Monaco's flag is almost identical to Indonesia's, but Monaco's is narrower.
The House of Grimaldi has ruled Monaco since 1297.

There is no airport in Monaco; residents use the airport in Nice, France.
Monaco has the world's most expensive real estate prices.
The 'Old Town' of Monaco is perched on a massive rock overlooking the sea.
Monaco is home to the world's largest luxury yacht show every September.
Andorra is the only country in the world where Catalan is the sole official language.

Andorra has no national bank and has never had its own currency.
The capital, Andorra la Vella, is the highest capital city in Europe.
Andorra has more museums per square kilometer than almost any other country.
The country has no airport and no railway system; it is only accessible by road.
Andorra has never had a national army or a ministry of defense.
90% of Andorra's territory is nature: forests, mountains, and lakes.
Andorra is a tax haven and is famous for its duty-free shopping.
It is one of the few countries where 10% of the land is a UNESCO World Heritage site.
Andorra remained neutral during both World War I and World War II.
Tobacco was traditionally the most important crop in Andorra's economy.
Andorra is the 16th smallest country in the world.

Andorra's parliament, the 'General Council', was established in 1419.
